<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kurozuka Kofun | native_name =黒塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kurozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.559925|N|135.843114|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196|1=Tenri, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11534332|1=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|33|35.73|N|135|50|35.21|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kurozuka Kofun'''|黒塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the Yanagimoto-cho neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri, Nara|1=Tenri, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2001.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192522|title=黒塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is noted for the 33 triangular-rimmed {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s with divine beasts that were excavated in almost the same arrangement as when they were originally buried.
==Overview== The Kurozuka Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It belongs to the Yanagimoto Kofun Group in the southeastern part of the Nara Basin, and is orientated to the west. The tumulus has a total length of about 130 meters. The posterior circular mound has a diameter about 72 meters and height of about dd meters and is constructed in three tiers. The anterior rectangular portion has a length of about 48 meters, height of 6 meters and is constructed in two tiers. A slight arc-shaped bulge can be seen in the front of the front circular mound, indicating that it is shaped like a drumstick. These are characteristics of early Kofun period. The tumulus has a moat. No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|WD=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|WD=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|WD=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|WD=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|WD=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|WD=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|WD=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|WD=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|WD=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|WD=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|WD=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|WD=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kurozuka Kofun | native_name =黒塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kurozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.559925|N|135.843114|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196|1=Tenri, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11534332|1=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|33|35.73|N|135|50|35.21|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kurozuka Kofun'''|黒塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the Yanagimoto-cho neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri, Nara|1=Tenri, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2001.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192522|title=黒塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is noted for the 33 triangular-rimmed {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s with divine beasts that were excavated in almost the same arrangement as when they were originally buried.
==Overview== The Kurozuka Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It belongs to the Yanagimoto Kofun Group in the southeastern part of the Nara Basin, and is orientated to the west. The tumulus has a total length of about 130 meters. The posterior circular mound has a diameter about 72 meters and height of about dd meters and is constructed in three tiers. The anterior rectangular portion has a length of about 48 meters, height of 6 meters and is constructed in two tiers. A slight arc-shaped bulge can be seen in the front of the front circular mound, indicating that it is shaped like a drumstick. These are characteristics of early Kofun period. The tumulus has a moat. No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|WD=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|WD=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|WD=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|WD=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|WD=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|WD=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|WD=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|WD=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|WD=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|WD=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|WD=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|WD=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kurozuka Kofun | native_name =黒塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kurozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.559925|N|135.843114|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196|1=Tenri, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11534332|1=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|33|35.73|N|135|50|35.21|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kurozuka Kofun'''|黒塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the Yanagimoto-cho neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri, Nara|1=Tenri, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2001.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192522|title=黒塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is noted for the 33 triangular-rimmed {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s with divine beasts that were excavated in almost the same arrangement as when they were originally buried.
==Overview== The Kurozuka Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It belongs to the Yanagimoto Kofun Group in the southeastern part of the Nara Basin, and is orientated to the west. The tumulus has a total length of about 130 meters. The posterior circular mound has a diameter about 72 meters and height of about dd meters and is constructed in three tiers. The anterior rectangular portion has a length of about 48 meters, height of 6 meters and is constructed in two tiers. A slight arc-shaped bulge can be seen in the front of the front circular mound, indicating that it is shaped like a drumstick. These are characteristics of early Kofun period. The tumulus has a moat. No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|WD=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|WD=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|WD=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|WD=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|WD=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|WD=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|WD=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|qid=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|qid=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|qid=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|qid=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|qid=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|qid=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|qid=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozuka Kofun]][[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kurozuka Kofun]][[en:Kurozuka Kofun]][[ja:黒塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kurozuka Kofun | native_name =黒塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kurozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.559925|N|135.843114|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Tenri|qid=Q502196|1=Tenri, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11534332|1=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|33|35.73|N|135|50|35.21|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kurozuka Kofun'''|黒塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, located in the Yanagimoto-cho neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri, Nara|1=Tenri, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2001.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192522|title=黒塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is noted for the 33 triangular-rimmed {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s with divine beasts that were excavated in almost the same arrangement as when they were originally buried.
==Overview== The Kurozuka Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It belongs to the Yanagimoto Kofun Group in the southeastern part of the Nara Basin, and is orientated to the west. The tumulus has a total length of about 130 meters. The posterior circular mound has a diameter about 72 meters and height of about dd meters and is constructed in three tiers. The anterior rectangular portion has a length of about 48 meters, height of 6 meters and is constructed in two tiers. A slight arc-shaped bulge can be seen in the front of the front circular mound, indicating that it is shaped like a drumstick. These are characteristics of early Kofun period. The tumulus has a moat. No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|qid=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|qid=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|qid=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|qid=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|qid=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|qid=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|qid=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|qid=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|qid=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. In the center of the posterior circular mound, there was a large-scale excavation pit from the Kamakura period that led to the burial chamber, destroying a portion of it. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|qid=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|qid=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|qid=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. As the Kurozuka Kofun was incorporated into the ''jin'ya'', the moat was repurposed and stone walls were constructed. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Archaeologist Taichiro Shiraishi has developed a chronological classification dating system for triangular-rimmed "god-beast" bronze mirrors, dividing by design into stages I to V. Per this system only stages I to III have been excavated from this tomb, and no stage IV or later has been excavated, so it is highly likely that the tumulus was built around 260 AD.
Since the Meiji period, most of the former ''jin'ya'' site has been the site of Tenri City Yanagimoto Elementary School, and the tomb has been developed by Tenri City to become Yanagimoto Park. Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|qid=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|qid=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|qid=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} | name = Kurozuka Kofun | native_name =黒塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kurozuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kurozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.559925|N|135.843114|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Tenri|qid=Q502196|1=Tenri, Nara}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of ={{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11534332|1=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster}} | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.3rd century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|33|35.73|N|135|50|35.21|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Kurozuka Kofun'''|黒塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|qid=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}}, located in the Yanagimoto-cho neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri, Nara|1=Tenri, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2001.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192522|title=黒塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is noted for the 33 triangular-rimmed {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s with divine beasts that were excavated in almost the same arrangement as when they were originally buried.
==Overview== The Kurozuka Kofun is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=[[Zenpō-kōen-fun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It belongs to the Yanagimoto Kofun Group in the southeastern part of the Nara Basin, and is orientated to the west. The tumulus has a total length of about 130 meters. The posterior circular mound has a diameter about 72 meters and height of about dd meters and is constructed in three tiers. The anterior rectangular portion has a length of about 48 meters, height of 6 meters and is constructed in two tiers. A slight arc-shaped bulge can be seen in the front of the front circular mound, indicating that it is shaped like a drumstick. These are characteristics of early Kofun period. The tumulus has a moat. No traces of ''[[fukiishi]]'' or ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-43dd750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During an [[archaeological excavation]] conducted by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute of Nara Prefecture from 1997 to 1998, 33 triangular-rimmed "god and beast" bronze mirrors and one slightly older "god and beast" bronze mirror with a painted and written belt were discovered in situ. The mirrors and other items were still in their original positions when they were buried, and the arrangement of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and their meanings became clear. In the northeast corner, an iron product of unknown purpose was leaning against the wall, consisting of two iron bars, one large and one small, bent into a U-shape. Between the two bars, there were several V-shaped iron pipes that had either attached or collapsed, and there was evidence that the large and small U-shaped iron bars had been connected to the pipes in a sawtooth pattern. A vermilion-painted wooden shield was leaning against the north side of the wooden coffin, facing north, as is common in early [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]] Kofuns.The north mirror outside the coffin is thought to have been placed on top of the shield. A group of arrowheads were found near the north end of the western side of the wooden coffin, with the tips facing south. It is believed that originally there was a bundle of arrow shafts in the gap. To the south of this, eleven swords, swords, and spears were placed together. Inside the coffin, a "picture-and-text belted divine beast" bronze mirror was placed at the head of the deceased, and one sword and one sword were placed on either side. Outside the wooden coffin, 15 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the east wall and 17 triangular-rimmed "divine beast" bronze mirrors were placed on the west wall with the mirrors facing inward, in the small space between the wooden coffin and the wall. The upper half of the deceased was surrounded by mirrors and swords in a U-shape, both inside and outside the coffin, and this arrangement is thought to have ritual significance. In addition, on the south side of the outside of the coffin, a large number of about 600 small metal plates, which are thought to have been a {{ill|coat of plates|en|coat of plates|qid=Q350324|lt=coat of plates|1=Coat of plates}} stomach bound with leather, axes and spear tools, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, were placed. No jewels or arm ornaments were found.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in the posterior circular mound is a vertical stone chamber with an internal length of about 8.3 meters, a northern entrance width of 0.9 meters, and a height of about 1.7 meters. The ceiling is in a ''gassho-zukuri'' style, and is made of slabs of stone from Kasugayama and Shibayama at the foot of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|qid=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. Inside the stone chamber, a clay coffin floor was set up, and a hollowed-out wooden coffin with a semicircular cross section and a total length of more than one meter was placed inside. The wooden coffin was painted with vermilion vermilion only in the central area, 2.8 meters long, and both ends were painted red with bengala.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In an earthquake thought to have occurred in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ceiling collapsed into the burial chamber, and the floor was covered with slabs of stone. However, as the chamber was largely was blocked by a large amount of fallen stones, and the grave robbers only entered the southern end. The grave goods were left intact except at the southern end of the burial chamber.In the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the Yanagimoto clan used the Kurozuka Kofun to build a fortification, but later fell into disuse. In 1575, it was again fortified, this time by {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, who built Yanagimoto Castle. After he rebelled against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and was killed, {{ill|Yanagimoto Domain|en|Yanagimoto Domain|qid=Q3034568|lt=Yanagimoto Domain|1=Yanagimoto Domain}} became the fief of Nobunaga's younger brother, {{ill|Oda Nagamasu|en|Oda Nagamasu|qid=Q837903|lt=Oda Nagamasu|1=Oda Nagamasu}}. During the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} era (1624–1643), Yanagimoto ''{{ill|Jin'ya|en|Jin'ya|qid=Q908908|lt=Jin'ya|1=Jin'ya}}'' was built on the site of Yanagimoto Castle and was used by the Oda clan]] as the Yanagimoto Domain's headquarters until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. The tumulus was modified, with a rectangular central bailey built at the top of the circular mound, and several flat enclosures were carved into the mound toward the front, forming a terraced area. A narrow, flat, curved wall was built to surround the rear mound, and a new moat measuring 6.8 meters wide and 3.4 meters deep was excavated at the lowest point of the front mound to function as a fortress. From the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, access to the tumulus was thus highly restricted, and this prevented subsequent grave robbing.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Adjacent to the tomb is the Tenri City Kurozuka Tomb Exhibition Hall, which displays a life-size model of the burial chamber and replicas of bronze mirrors. The excavated items were designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 2004, and are owned by the Kashihara Archaeological Institute.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207560|title=奈良県黒塚古墳出土品|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
<gallery> 080515 Kuroduka Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view </gallery>
The site is about a five-minute walk from {{ill|Yanagimoto Station|en|Yanagimoto Station|qid=Q4188827|lt=Yanagimoto Station|1=Yanagimoto Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|en|Sakurai Line|qid=Q865801|lt=Sakurai Line|1=Sakurai Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
